annotations.label: Sum of two angles of a triangle (Elements I.17)

anchor.snippet: two angles taken together in any manner are less than two right angles

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the triangle angle sum equals π and each angle is positive, which together imply I.17, but the explicit 'two angles sum to less than π' statement is not stated.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem (Elements I.47)

anchor.snippet: In right-angled triangles the square on the side subtending the right angle

annotations.note: Directly states that the squared hypotenuse equals the sum of squared legs iff the angle is a right angle.

annotations.label: Infinitude of primes

anchor.snippet: It is proved that there are infinitely many prime numbers.

annotations.note: States that for every n there is a prime p ≥ n, i.e. infinitely many primes.

annotations.label: Cone–cylinder volume ratio 1:3

anchor.snippet: the 1:3 ratio between the volume of a cone and a cylinder with the same height and base

annotations.note: No declaration computing or comparing the volumes of a cone and cylinder is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Postulate 1 (line through two points)

anchor.snippet: To draw a straight line from any point to any point.

annotations.note: Mathlib has the affine span of two points (the line through them) but does not axiomatize Euclid's postulate synthetically.

annotations.label: Postulate 2 (extend a line)

anchor.snippet: To produce (extend) a finite straight line continuously in a straight line.

annotations.note: Mathlib builds geometry analytically and has no statement of segment extensibility as a postulate.

annotations.label: Postulate 3 (circle with any center and radius)

anchor.snippet: To describe a circle with any centre and distance (radius).

annotations.note: Spheres/circles with arbitrary center and radius exist as objects (EuclideanGeometry.Sphere over Metric.sphere) but the postulate itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Postulate 4 (all right angles equal)

anchor.snippet: That all right angles are equal to one another.

annotations.note: A right angle is defined as π/2 so equality is definitional; there is no stated postulate-form theorem.

annotations.label: Postulate 5 (parallel postulate)

anchor.snippet: if a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles

annotations.note: Mathlib does not axiomatize Euclid's fifth postulate.

annotations.label: Common notions

anchor.snippet: Things that are equal to the same thing are also equal to one another

annotations.note: Individual common notions like transitivity of equality are core (Eq.trans), but the bundle is not formalized as Euclid's common notions.

annotations.label: Playfair's axiom

anchor.snippet: through a point not on a given straight line, at most one line can be drawn that never meets the given line

annotations.note: No formalization of Playfair's uniqueness-of-parallels axiom is present.

annotations.label: Complementary angles

anchor.snippet: Angles whose sum is a right angle are called complementary

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated definition of complementary angles.

annotations.label: Supplementary angles

anchor.snippet: Angles whose sum is a straight angle are supplementary

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated definition of supplementary angles.

annotations.label: Pons asinorum (overview)

anchor.section: Some important or well known results
anchor.snippet: The pons asinorum or bridge of asses theorem states that in an isosceles triangle

annotations.note: States that equal sides from the apex give equal base angles, the isosceles triangle theorem.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum (overview)

anchor.section: Some important or well known results
anchor.snippet: the sum of the three angles of any triangle

annotations.note: States the three interior angles of a (non-degenerate) triangle sum to π.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem (overview)

anchor.section: Some important or well known results
anchor.snippet: the sum of the areas of the two squares on the legs

annotations.note: Same right-angle squared-distance characterization gives the Pythagorean relation.

annotations.label: Thales' theorem (overview)

anchor.section: Some important or well known results
anchor.snippet: if AC is a diameter, then the angle at B is a right angle

annotations.note: Aliased as thales_theorem: the angle inscribed in a semicircle (on a diameter) is a right angle.

annotations.label: Pons asinorum

anchor.snippet: in isosceles triangles the angles at the base equal one another

annotations.note: States that an isosceles triangle's two base angles are equal.

annotations.label: Triangle congruence criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA)

anchor.snippet: Triangles are congruent if they have all three sides equal (SSS)

annotations.note: Mathlib proves SSS (side_side_side), SAS (side_angle_side), and ASA (angle_side_angle) congruence criteria.

annotations.label: AAA gives similarity not congruence

anchor.snippet: Triangles with three equal angles (AAA) are similar, but not necessarily congruent.

annotations.note: Proves equal angles imply similar triangles (similarity, not congruence), matching the AAA claim.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum theorem

anchor.snippet: The sum of the angles of a triangle is equal to a straight angle (180 degrees).

annotations.note: States the interior angles of a triangle sum to π (a straight angle).

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: in any right triangle, the area of the square whose side is the hypotenuse

annotations.note: Characterizes the right angle by the squared-hypotenuse-equals-sum-of-squared-legs identity.

annotations.label: Thales' theorem

anchor.snippet: if A, B, and C are points on a circle where the line AC is a diameter of the circle, then the angle ABC is a right angle

annotations.note: Directly formalizes Thales' theorem about the right angle subtended by a diameter.

annotations.label: Area scales as square of linear dimension

anchor.snippet: the area of a plane figure is proportional to the square of any of its linear dimensions

annotations.note: Proves μ(r • s) = |r|^(finrank) · μ(s), which for dimension 2 gives the square-scaling of area as a special case.

annotations.label: Volume scales as cube of linear dimension

anchor.snippet: the volume of a solid to the cube

annotations.note: Same Haar-measure scaling law μ(r • s) = |r|^(finrank) · μ(s) gives the cube-scaling of volume in dimension 3.

annotations.label: Sphere is 2/3 volume of circumscribing cylinder

anchor.snippet: a sphere has 2/3 the volume of the circumscribing cylinder

annotations.note: Mathlib has no statement of the sphere-to-cylinder volume ratio.

annotations.label: Congruence of figures

anchor.snippet: two figures are congruent if one can be moved on top of the other so that it matches up with it exactly

annotations.note: Defines Congruent as distance-preserving correspondence between indexed point sets, the metric notion of congruence.

annotations.label: Similarity of figures

anchor.snippet: Figures that would be congruent except for their differing sizes are referred to as similar

annotations.note: Defines Similar as proportionality of all distances between indexed point sets.

annotations.label: Archimedean property

anchor.snippet: enunciated the Archimedean property of finite numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib's Archimedean typeclass states that for any x there exists n with n • 1 ≥ x, the standard Archimedean property.

annotations.label: Euclidean metric

anchor.snippet: is then known as the Euclidean metric

annotations.note: Gives the L2 distance formula on EuclideanSpace, i.e. the Euclidean metric.

annotations.label: Affine geometry

anchor.snippet: a generalization of Euclidean geometry called affine geometry

annotations.note: AffineSpace (notation for AddTorsor) and a full affine geometry library exist, capturing the field but not a single 'affine geometry' statement.

annotations.label: Impossibility of angle trisection

anchor.snippet: Pierre Wantzel published a proof in 1837 that such a construction was impossible

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theory of compass-and-straightedge constructible numbers proving angle trisection impossible.

annotations.label: Impossibility of doubling the cube

anchor.snippet: doubling the cube

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of the impossibility of doubling the cube with compass and straightedge.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: squaring the circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has Real.transcendental_pi but no derivation of the impossibility of squaring the circle as a constructibility result.

annotations.label: Number of regular convex polytopes by dimension

anchor.snippet: there are six regular convex polytopes in dimension four , and three in all higher dimensions

annotations.note: Mathlib does not classify or count regular convex polytopes.

annotations.label: Independence of the parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: the parallel postulate cannot be proved from the other postulates

annotations.note: No formalization of the independence (unprovability) of the parallel postulate exists.

annotations.label: A line segment is part of a triangle

anchor.snippet: any line segment is part of a triangle

annotations.note: No such statement embedding a segment in a triangle is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Law of contradiction

anchor.snippet: if S is any statement, then S and a contradiction (that is, the denial) of S cannot both hold

annotations.note: The lemma and_not_self_iff : (a ∧ ¬a) ↔ False expresses non-contradiction, though it is a core/Batteries lemma rather than Mathlib-defined.

annotations.label: Law of the excluded middle

anchor.snippet: either S or the denial of S must hold

annotations.note: em (alias of Classical.em) states p ∨ ¬p for every proposition.

annotations.label: Completeness/decidability of elementary Euclidean geometry (Tarski)

anchor.snippet: there is an algorithm that, for every proposition, can be shown either true or false

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Tarski's completeness/decidability result for elementary Euclidean geometry.
